text: This video presents a technique for laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with extended lymphadenectomy. Previous reports proposed an oncologic benefit with a more radical lymphadenectomy in colon cancer surgery. However, while the technique was more widely adopted in left colon surgery, right-sided extended lymphadenectomy did not gain much popularity.
A possible explanation is the more complex vascular anatomy of the right colon mesentery. Thus, initial focus has been on open methods.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy using a medial-to-lateral, caudal-to-cranial approach, focusing on the technical steps required for extended lymphadenectomy.
The case is that of a forty-six-year-old male with acecal cancer.

The surgeon retracts the ileocolic vessels towards the right iliac fossa while simultaneously retracting the transverse colon and omentum cranially, creating exposure for the laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon opens the right colon mesentery posterior to the ileocolic vessels, performing blunt dissection to separate the mesentery from the retroperitoneal fascia while preserving the visceral mesenteric envelope. The peritoneum over the superior mesenteric vein is opened from the iliocolic vessels towards the middle colic artery, exposing the superior mesenteric vein for extended lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon retracts the ileocolic vessels anteriorly, extending the retro mesocolic dissection medially until the head of the pancreas is exposed. The increased length of the ileocolic vessels is visible.

The surgeon exposes the ileocolic vein at its insertion point to the superior mesenteric vein. The ileocolic artery, which typically passes underneath the superior mesenteric vein, is not visible in this region.

The surgeon performs a high division of the ileocolic vein and artery, continuing the dissection towards the middle colic artery, maintaining a distance of two to three centimeters from the origin of the ileocolic vessels.
The surgeon exposes the gastrocolic trunk of Henle located ventral to the pancreas head, revealing an additional deep mesenteric lymph node.

The surgeon dissects the middle colic artery, harvesting an additional lymph node. The middle colic vein and the right branch of the middle colic artery are divided at their origin, completing the vascular division in this area.

The surgeon divides the transverse colon and ileum, releasing the colon from its lateral attachments. Upon completion of the resection, the mesocolon is fully dissected off the retroperitoneum, revealing the ureter, gonadal vessels, duodenum, and pancreas head.

The gastrocolic vein and middle colic artery are visible in the surgical field. The surgeon aligns the ileum and transverse colon in an isoperistaltic manner and performs an intracorporeal anastomosis to connect these sections of the bowel.

The specimen is extracted through a five-centimeter transverse suprapubic incision. Forceps grasp the abscess of the mesenteric vessels. A red line indicates the mesenteric transection line, while a red circle marks the site of additional lymph node retrieval. Blue lines outline the complete, unviolated mesentery, confirming proper anatomical dissection.

The post-operative course was uneventful, and the patient was discharged home on the fifth day. On pathology, the tumor was classified as T-III-N-II-B. Thirty-four lymph nodes were extracted, and nine were involved. Both apical nodes of the iliocolic and middle colic arteries were not involved by cancer.
